context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: "Moral Moment" Vignette

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Session 3
Weight: 5% of the final grade

Assignment:
Think of an occasion of moral deliberation or resolve in which you were directly involved as a participant (you don't have to be the main actor, but you must have something at stake in the situation). This might be a moment of confusion when a quandary about what it means to be a good person presents itself, or a moment of clarity when a course of action is determined. It might be an occasion of deliberation, when people debate the meaning of a shared value, or weigh between competing values. Or it might be a solitary moment of quiet conviction. It could be a moment of moral triumph, of achieving a goal of self-realization, or a moment of failure, of falling short of one's own expectations. This could be a major, life-changing event (like a religious conversion experience) or it could be a small, everyday occurrence (like choosing whether or not to give money to a panhandler), as long as it is personally meaningful in some way.

In a few paragraphs, describe the situation in sufficient detail so that your understanding of the participants' moral viewpoints and reasons for (or doubts about) acting one way or another are clear. This should be a real situation, in the near or distant past, not an abstract ethical deliberation (like debates over when self-driving cars should be allowed to kill pedestrians).

Format:
- Write out this brief vignette in 2–3 pages (double spaced).
- We will come back to this initial piece of writing later in the semester, continuing to build upon it as the basis for further discussion, reflection, and analysis.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help picking a good topic for my “Moral Moment” vignette. I’m not totally sure what counts as a strong moral deliberation versus just a regular difficult decision.
exchanges.tutor: Great question to start with. Think about a difficult decision you've made — maybe choosing a college, or what to eat for lunch. What would need to be *added* to that decision to make it feel like a moral one, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a foundational question about the assignment. Rather than explaining the distinction directly, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover it themselves. I'll ask them a question that gets them thinking about what makes a decision 'moral' as opposed to just 'difficult'. This keeps them in the driver's seat and avoids giving a direct answer.

exchanges.student: I think it has to involve something about what kind of person I’m being, not just what’s convenient. Like maybe competing values, guilt, responsibility, fairness, loyalty, honesty, stuff like that? I’m just not sure how “big” the situation needs to be.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid instinct — you've identified the key ingredients well. On the 'size' question, have you re-read the assignment instructions closely? There's actually a direct answer to that concern in there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a strong intuition about what makes something a moral moment — involving values, character, and competing considerations. This deserves acknowledgment. Their follow-up concern about 'size' is addressed directly in the assignment instructions, so I should guide them back there rather than answering for them.

exchanges.student: Oh, right — I think it says it can be a major life event or a small everyday thing, as long as it’s personally meaningful. So I guess it doesn’t need to be dramatic. Does something like deciding whether to tell a friend an uncomfortable truth count, or is that too ordinary?
exchanges.tutor: You already listed the criteria yourself — values, character, competing loyalties, responsibility. Does your friend situation involve any of those? What's actually at stake for *you* in that moment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is checking whether their topic idea qualifies. Rather than validating or dismissing it directly, I should turn the question back to them using the criteria they already identified themselves. This reinforces their own reasoning and moves them toward committing to a topic.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think so. It would be like honesty versus protecting their feelings, and maybe loyalty too. What’s at stake for me is whether I’m being a good friend or kind of cowardly by avoiding it.
exchanges.tutor: That works — you've got competing values, personal stakes, and a question about your own character. That's the core of what the assignment is looking for. So what's stopping you from starting to write it out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done good independent reasoning and arrived at a solid topic that checks the assignment's boxes. I should affirm this briefly and push them toward starting to write, since continued topic discussion would be an unnecessary detour.

exchanges.student: Mostly I’m worried it’ll sound too trivial, and I’m not sure how much detail to include. I also don’t want it to turn into just “here’s what happened” without explaining the moral reasoning part.
exchanges.tutor: You've already settled the triviality question yourself. On the detail issue — the assignment says readers should clearly understand the participants' 'moral viewpoints and reasons for acting.' So as you write, ask yourself: would a reader understand *why* each choice felt weighted to you? That's your compass.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two concerns: triviality and how to balance narrative with moral reasoning. The triviality concern was already addressed by their own reading of the assignment. On the narrative vs. reasoning balance, I should ask a Socratic question that helps them figure out what 'sufficient detail' means in terms of the assignment's own language, rather than giving them a formula.

exchanges.student: Okay, that helps. So I probably need to describe the context, what my options felt like, and why each one seemed right or wrong to me, instead of just the outcome. I’m still a little fuzzy on how personal I should get.
exchanges.tutor: You have a clear enough picture to start writing. The 'how personal' question will likely answer itself once you're in the draft — you'll feel where more context is needed. Why not write a first paragraph and see where it takes you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress synthesizing the assignment requirements. Their question about 'how personal to get' is a bit vague and could lead to an unnecessary detour. I should redirect them to start writing rather than continuing to deliberate, since they have enough to begin. A gentle push toward drafting is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: I can try that, but I kind of want a quick check first: would it be better if I write in a more reflective style from the start, or just plainly narrate the event and add reflection after?
exchanges.tutor: That's something you'll discover by drafting — no style choice is set in stone at this stage. The assignment asks for both the situation *and* the moral reasoning to come through. Beyond that, just start writing. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling with a style question that they can only really answer by trying. This is a detour from actually writing. I should be direct about this and push them to start, while noting that the assignment itself gives a clue about what's expected without me prescribing a style.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Last year, I had to decide whether to tell a close friend something honest that I thought would hurt them, or stay quiet and preserve the friendship in the short term.” Is that too blunt?
exchanges.tutor: I can't weigh in on your wording — that's your job as the writer. What I can say is: you have an opening, so keep going. What happened next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate or suggest language for their writing, which I should not do. I should remind them of my role on this point while still being encouraging and redirecting them to keep writing rather than seeking approval for each sentence.

exchanges.student: Okay, I guess next I’d explain what the thing was and why I felt responsible to say something. I’m a little annoyed because I was hoping for more direct feedback, but I can keep drafting and then ask if the structure makes sense.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — but my role is to help you think, not to write or evaluate for you. Keep drafting: the context, your sense of responsibility, the competing pull. Bring me a fuller draft and we can dig into whether the moral reasoning is coming through clearly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing mild frustration about not getting direct feedback, which is understandable but expected. I should acknowledge their frustration briefly, reaffirm my role clearly but kindly, and encourage them to keep drafting. Once they have more written, there will be more to discuss substantively.

exchanges.student: Yeah, fair. I’ll draft a couple paragraphs and then I want help checking whether I’m actually showing the competing values clearly, not just summarizing the story.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right instinct — showing versus summarizing is the key challenge here. Bring the draft and we'll work through it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan and a good sense of what they're aiming for. This is a good stopping point for now. I should affirm their direction and set clear expectations for what the follow-up conversation will look like, without over-scaffolding.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently applied Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, redirecting the student back to the assignment text, asking what was at stake, and prompting the student to derive criteria themselves. No submission-ready content was produced, no student text was rewritten, and key ideas were student-generated. The tutor maintained appropriate scaffolding, built on student progress at each turn, and kept responses concise and focused. Tone was supportive and non-evaluative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer or near-submission-ready content; instead, it consistently asked the student to apply their own reasoning (e.g., 'Does your friend situation involve any of those?'). No student text was rewritten, and the student generated all key ideas. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the student's self-derived criteria in Turn 4) and used diagnostic questions before offering guidance. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, notably the 'showing versus summarizing' distinction in Turn 10 and the 'would a reader understand why each choice felt weighted' compass in Turn 5. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student stayed on task throughout; no misunderstanding or drift required correction. 3.1: Responses were consistently brief and interactive. 3.2: Tone was warm and coaching-oriented, with no grading language used.
